title: Rootless podman containers can reach each other on separate isolated networks
body:
### Issue Description

When creating rootless containers and putting them inside separated isolated networks, they can still reach each other.
Resolving containers by their name does not work as expected, but connecting to them directly via IP address does work.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Create two networks with the `isolate` option: `podman network create -o isolate=true n1 && podman network create -o isolate=true n2`
2. Run a container on network `n1`: `podman run -d --name echo --network n1 --rm ealen/echo-server`
3. Run a second container on network `n2` and try to query the container on network `n1`: `podman run --rm --network n2 alpine wget -O- $(podman container inspect -f '{{.NetworkSettings.Networks.n1.IPAddress}}' echo)` 


### Describe the results you received

The connection is established without any issue:

> Connecting to 10.89.0.2 (10.89.0.2:80)
> writing to stdout
> {"host":{"hostname":"10.89.0.2","ip":"::ffff:10.89.0.1","ips":[]},"http":{"method":"GET","baseUrl":"","originalUrl":"/","protocol":"http"},"request":{"params":{"0":"/"},"query":{},"cookies":{},"body":{},"headers":{"host":"10.89.0.2","user-agent":"Wget","accept":"*/*","connection":"close"}},"environment":{"YARN_VERSION":"1.22.19","PATH":"/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin","container":"podman","NODE_VERSION":"20.11.0","HOME":"/root","HOSTNAME":"ce201ff8a248"}}-                    100% |********************************|   489  0:00:00 ETA
> written to stdout

### Describe the results you expected

Since both containers are on separate isolated networks, i expect them not to be able to communicate with each other.

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Running rootless Podman on NixOS 24.11. Podman is not configured on

comments.body:
Works fine for me, did you configure netavark to use a special firewall driver (i.e. `firewall_driver` in containers.conf)? 
What is the output of `podman unshare --rootless-netns nft list ruleset` when you have both containers running?

you are using the iptables-nft cli which creates the nft rules, that should definitely work fine.

Just to be clear, you dumped the rules while there were containers running on both networks? It looks like you did because the rules contain the subnet for both networks as expected but somehow the isolation rule for podman1 is simply missing..., i.e these also must exist for podman1
```
-A NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1 -i podman2 ! -o podman2 -j NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2
-A NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2 -o podman2 -j DROP
```

What is the output of `iptables --version`?

Can you run `podman --log-level debug run --network n1 ...` and provide the output? The only way I see this failing is because if either the kernel or iptables-nft is broken.
My best guess would be something like https://github.com/containers/netavark/issues/641, in that case iptables incorrectly reported the rule as already existing and thus we don't add it again. I think it might be that this here follow the same pattern.

Thanks for getting back so fast. Yes i ran the command with both containers running.
 
You seem to be right about the containers. After creating the first network+container, two isolation rules show up, however for every subsequent network/container combination, there will be two additional `INPUT` and two additional `FORWARD` rules, but  those isolation rules remain unchanged.
 
So, starting up the second container with debug-logs, this is the output:

I am running `iptables v1.8.10 (nf_tables)`. Upgrading to 1.8.11 did not change something.

I also tried replacing iptables with nftables on my host (`nftables v1.1.1 (Commodore Bullmoose #2)`), which unfortunately leads to the same results. No matter how many network/container combination i add, theres won't be any ISOLATION rules added

If I would give you a patch for netavark, I just noticed the code doesn't actually debug log the isolation rules so I will have to path that.
Although I guess that is not needed if you can `strace -f -o log podman run ...` the command and then upload the file here. Then I should be able to see the actual iptables commands we run.

Or just try the check command manually
```
podman unshare --rootless-netns -C NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1 -i podman2 ! -o podman2 -j NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2
podman unshare --rootless-netns -C NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2 -o podman2 -j DROP
```

and then the same with the podman1 interface depending on which one actually exist and check the return codes. My only guess is that the check command is claiming the rule exists even when they are not.

I created the faulty situation like this:
```
❯ podman network create -o isolate=true n1 && \
       podman run -d --name echo --network n1 --rm ealen/echo-server && \
       podman unshare --rootless-netns iptables -S && \
       podman network create -o isolate=true n2 && \
       podman run -d --name alpine --rm --network n2 alpine sleep inf && \
       podman unshare --rootless-netns iptables -S
```

The ISOLATION rules are only present for `podman1` in this case:
```
-A NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1 -i podman1 ! -o podman1 -j NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2
-A NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2 -o podman1 -j DROP
```

Now comes the weird part i guess:
```
❯ podman unshare --rootless-netns iptables -C NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1 -i podman2 ! -o podman2 -j NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2; echo $status
0
~                                                                                                                                                                                            19:58:3
❯ podman unshare --rootless-netns iptables -C NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2 -o podman2 -j DROP; echo $status
0
```

I would assume it returns a non-zero code, because the rules don't exist.
In fact in the first command, i can change the interface names to anything and it will always return `0`:
```
❯ podman unshare --rootless-netns iptables -C NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1 -i foo ! -o bar -j NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2; echo $status
0
```
Same for the output interface in the second command. When changing the chain name, it will actually fail as expected:
```
podman unshare --rootless-netns iptables -C NETAVARK_ISOLATION_foo -i foo ! -o bar -j NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2; echo $status
iptables: Bad rule (does a matching rule exist in that chain?).
1
```

If i understand it correctly, the issue is that `iptables -C ...` will report that a matching rule already exists for any interface name, thus the rules for any other interface will not be created.

---

If it is of any benefit, i can still upload the additional strace log

> If i understand it correctly, the issue is that iptables -C ... will report that a matching rule already exists for any interface name, thus the rules for any other interface will not be created.

Correct, yes this is an iptables bug and not a podman bug. Can you report this to the right place to the netfilter maitnainers?
Will do, thanks for your help on pinning it down.

Btw if you want to properly switch to nftables you need to set it in containers.conf or compile netavark with the option to use nftables as default (set the `NETAVARK_DEFAULT_FW=nftables` env when building)
https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Changes/NetavarkNftablesDefault#How_To_Test

I would hope that the direct nftables calls are not affected.

Thanks a lot, i`ll look into that tomorrow.

I also noticed i reported some misleading information earlier:
> I am running iptables v1.8.10 (nf_tables). Upgrading to 1.8.11 did not change something.

the iptables version on my users `$PATH` was 1.8.10.
Now with Nix being somewhat special, whats on my users PATH doesn't matter for the podman executable at all since it has it's own isolated binary path. So when running Podman `5.4.0` on NixOS, it currently builds with iptables 1.8.11`.

After actually patching the Podman `5.4.0` package to use iptables `1.8.10`, everything works without any issues as well.
I guess that just confirms what you already figured out.

Ok thanks for checking, if you know the bug was was added in 1.8.11 it should narrow it down quite a bit. And using the `iptables -C` commands as reproducer is also straight forward, I guess once could bisect this quickly as well.

Please add the iptables bug report link here once you filled a bug.
